title: Research
text:
My current book project, Affective Drift and Englishness from Woolf to Brexit: Literature, Film, and Media, examines how Englishness has been shaped through emotional practices of restraint, endurance, and belonging. The project traces changing forms of English identity from modernist literature to contemporary heritage media and post-Brexit culture.
My broader research explores diaspora narratives, postcolonial experiences of feeling, heritage genres, and streaming media. I study how cultural forms organize experiences of memory, belonging, and social difference.

title: Teaching
text: I view literature and cultural studies as opportunities for students to examine how narratives shape ideas of identity, history, and social experience. My teaching emphasizes close reading, critical discussion, and the relationship between literary texts, visual media, and contemporary cultural questions. I have gained extensive experience as an Instructor of Record at Binghamton University, and I am currently based in Seoul, where I continue to teach literature and media studies.

title: Bora Kang
text:
Bora Kang
Ph.D. in English

Contact
📧 bkang21@binghamton.edu
🎓 Google Scholar

title: Bora Kang
text:
I am a scholar of Anglophone literature specializing in modern and contemporary British literature, film and media, visual studies, and cultural studies. I received my Ph.D. in English from Binghamton University. My research examines how literary and cultural forms shape affective experience across national belonging, collective memory, and cultural identity.
My work traces how affective configurations, expressed across the twentieth-century British novel and in heritage media, have shaped Englishness and national identity over time. My current project develops this research into a monograph, Affective Drift and Englishness from Woolf to Brexit: Literature, Film, and Media.
This inquiry has since expanded toward affective experience among non-Western and marginalized subjects, tracing how emotion circulates unevenly within diasporic and postcolonial narratives. A parallel strand of my work examines how diverse media—including streaming platforms and physical sites like museums—mediate affective experiences and shape the social imaginaries that define contemporary life.
